topic: The Eclipse Test: Not What It Appears — Conclusion Section
our_argument_summary: Section 6.2 is a two-paragraph conclusion-section summary of the eclipse analysis. Paragraph 1 restates the core argument from Section 3.2: the dome misrepresents the globe prediction as '0.0 nT exactly,' the dome's −17 to −21 nT range was derived by applying a 1.672× scaling factor to actual INTERMAGNET data, and the test is heads-I-win, tails-doesn't-count. Paragraph 2 discusses the repository source code: monitor.py hardcodes Kp < 2 precondition, records pass=null (not pass=false) when conditions are disturbed, ~60% of days have Kp ≥ 2 giving a built-in excuse rate, and the code computes −29.1 nT rather than the advertised −17 to −21 nT.

holes_found.description: Section 6.2 inherits and repeats the factual errors identified in the SEC-3.2 review. Most critically: (1) The '−17 to −21 nT' range does not accurately represent the dome's current prediction structure, which is a dual-baseline, 9-station system. The BOU baseline gives −5.0 to −12.8 nT; the W004 baseline gives −10.3 to −26.2 nT. Our compressed '−17 to −21 nT' doesn't match either baseline. (2) The '1.672× scaling factor' is described incorrectly — 1.672 is the KAPPA magnetic-gravity coupling constant from WIN-012 (10.9 nT / 6.5 μGal), NOT the factor used to derive eclipse predictions. The actual eclipse formula is delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF, where FSF varies by station (0.642 to 2.075). These are different numbers and different concepts. A dome defender will immediately point out we're confusing KAPPA with FSF, undermining our credibility on the very section we frame as a key conclusion.
holes_found.recommendation: Update both Section 6.2 and Section 3.2 to accurately reflect the dual-baseline, per-station prediction structure. Replace the '1.672× correction factor' language with a correct description of the delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF formula. The critique that the predictions overlap with Chapman's range still holds (especially for the BOU baseline), but accuracy about what we're critiquing is non-negotiable.

holes_found.description: The claim that 'the code computes −29.1 nT — not the −17 to −21 nT advertised on the homepage' is presented without showing the derivation. Where does −29.1 come from? If it's from applying the W004 baseline formula to a specific station (e.g., −22.24 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −35.3 nT, or perhaps −22.24 × 0.95 × 1.029 ≈ −21.7 nT for Ebro), neither of those equals −29.1. If the computation is real and verifiable in monitor.py source code, we should show the exact line and derivation. If we cannot verify it (since monitor.py is not in our raw-text/ directory and the file doesn't exist locally), this claim is unverifiable and should either be sourced precisely or qualified with 'based on analysis of the dome repository's monitoring code.' A dome defender can demand 'show me the line in monitor.py that computes −29.1' and if we can't produce it, this is a fabricated-citation-class error.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) locate and cite the exact computation in the dome's repository code that produces −29.1, showing the formula and values, or (b) replace with the verifiable computation from the predictions page: e.g., 'The W004 baseline predictions for Hartland (−26.2 nT) significantly exceed the −17 to −21 nT range advertised on the homepage, revealing internal inconsistency between what's presented to the public and what the detailed predictions actually say.'

holes_found.description: Section 6.2 completely ignores the dome's four model-discriminating tests (E-PRED-A through E-PRED-D), which are the dome's strongest counterargument. E-PRED-B (Hartland beats Ebro despite lower coverage) is derived from dome-specific FSF geometry and makes a RELATIVE prediction that standard Chapman physics doesn't trivially predict. By omitting these tests in the conclusion, we leave our flank completely exposed. A dome defender reading our conclusion would say: 'They only address the absolute nT range and completely ignore our model-discriminating tests — they're arguing against a strawman.' This is especially damaging in a conclusion section which is supposed to summarize the strongest version of the argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence acknowledging the four E-PRED tests and explaining why they don't save the prediction. Possible framing: 'The dome's four model-discriminating sub-tests (including the Hartland-vs-Ebro relative ranking) are the strongest element of the prediction, but standard ionospheric physics predicts station-specific effects that depend on local conductivity, Sq current geometry, and latitude — not just eclipse coverage fraction. The relative ranking is not uniquely dome-derived.'

holes_found.description: The HTML version of Section 6.2 (Paragraph 2) claims 'roughly 60% of days have Kp ≥ 2.' This statistic is plausible but uncited. The Kp index is a 3-hour planetary index ranging 0–9. During solar minimum, median Kp is around 1–2; during solar maximum, it's higher. In 2026 (near solar maximum), the percentage of days with ALL eight 3-hour Kp values < 2 is likely well below 40%, but the percentage of days where the eclipse-hours Kp < 2 could be quite different. The claim conflates daily-average and instantaneous Kp. A dome defender could counter with specific Kp statistics from NOAA showing that the Kp < 2 condition during specific 6-hour windows is met more than 40% of the time. The claim needs precision or qualification.
holes_found.recommendation: Either cite specific NOAA Kp distribution data (e.g., from the SWPC definitive Kp archive) for the relevant solar cycle phase, or soften the language to 'a significant fraction of days, particularly during solar maximum.' Also clarify whether the condition applies to the full day or the eclipse-window hours specifically.

holes_found.description: The DOCX version (build-doc-v4.js line 794) says 'as documented in Part 3.1' when it should reference Part 3.2 (the eclipse analysis section). Part 3.1 is the 9.2-sigma dashboard analysis, not the eclipse test. This cross-reference error means the DOCX directs readers to the wrong section.

The DOCX version of Section 6.2 has three paragraphs with a different structure than the HTML's two paragraphs. The DOCX omits the monitor.py source code analysis (Kp < 2 escape clause, pass=null, 60% excuse rate, −29.1 nT computation) that appears in the HTML version. The DOCX adds a third paragraph about what a 'genuinely discriminating eclipse test' would look like, which doesn't appear in the HTML Section 6.2 (though similar language exists in Section 3.2). This divergence means the HTML and DOCX versions are arguing slightly different points, which is a maintenance hazard and consistency issue.
holes_found.recommendation: Align the HTML and DOCX versions. The source-code analysis (monitor.py findings) is a strong argument that belongs in both versions. The 'genuinely discriminating test' paragraph is good content that also belongs in both.

holes_found.description: The Kp escape clause criticism, while structurally valid, is somewhat uncharitable without context. Quiet-condition filtering is standard practice in geomagnetic studies. Chernogor & Holub (2024) applied Kp ≤ 3 filtering in their own eclipse study. Calling it an 'escape clause' without acknowledging mainstream science uses the same practice risks looking like we're selectively criticizing standard methodology when the dome uses it. The SEC-3.2 curmudgeon review already flagged this issue.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief qualifier: 'While quiet-condition filtering is standard in geomagnetic research, the concern is whether pass/fail criteria and Kp data sources are committed in advance — or whether the dome reserves discretion to retroactively reclassify conditions after seeing unfavorable results.'

holes_found.description: The HTML version mentions 'the March 9, 2016 Pacific eclipse (station GUA)' as the source of the dome's baseline data. However, the dome's current predictions page shows BOU 2017 (−10.9 nT) and W004 empirical (−22.24 nT) as baselines — GUA 2016 does not appear. Either we're describing an obsolete version of the prediction or we've confused the source station. The SEC-3.2 review already flagged this discrepancy.
holes_found.recommendation: Update to reflect current baselines (BOU 2017 and W004 empirical) or explicitly note that we're describing an earlier version's derivation.

stronger_arguments.description: The dual-baseline strategy is a powerful hedging mechanism that Section 6.2 should call out explicitly. BOU baseline covers −5 to −13 nT (entirely within Chapman's range — non-discriminating). W004 baseline covers −10 to −26 nT (overlapping at the low end, exceeding at the high end). Combined, the prediction spans −5 to −26 nT with error bars. Since the Kp condition excludes near-zero results, the effective prediction covers essentially the entire physically plausible quiet-day eclipse signal space. This is a much more devastating structural critique than our current framing.

The W004 baseline (−22.24 nT) provenance deserves scrutiny in the conclusion. According to the evolution page (v50.2), W004 was an 'empirical recalibration' — originally a failed weekly test below the detection threshold, repurposed as a baseline. The dome's stronger prediction set is built on a data point it previously classified as noise-floor failure. The provenance chain: failed test → relabeled as 'empirical baseline' → multiplied across 9 stations → presented as precise predictions with ±error bars. This deserves at least a sentence in the conclusion.

Section 6.2 should note that this is the dome's THIRD eclipse test attempt. WIN-025 (2024 eclipse) was REMOVED due to storm contamination and timing scatter. PROS-003 was SUSPENDED due to uncorrelated Z minima. The August 2026 test is attempt #3 with the same underlying claim. Two prior failures followed by a third attempt with a wider prediction range and more stations is a significant pattern. Section 3.5.7 mentions this but Section 6.2 (the conclusion) does not.

kernel_of_truth.description: The core argument of Section 6.2 is correct: the eclipse test as framed is not genuinely discriminating because the dome's predicted range overlaps with what the Chapman mechanism already predicts, the globe prediction is misrepresented as 0.0 nT, and the Kp precondition provides an escape hatch. The 'heads-I-win, tails-doesn't-count' framing is an accurate structural description of the test design. The monitor.py source code analysis (if verifiable) adds a forensic dimension that strengthens the argument. These are sound observations that would survive adversarial scrutiny if the factual details were corrected.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: N/A — this is a review of our own section, not a dome claim. The kernel here is that our core argument is right even though the supporting details contain errors.

deeper_analysis:
Section 6.2 is a conclusion-section summary of the eclipse analysis — it needs to be the tightest, most accurate version of the argument since it's the last thing readers encounter on this topic before the final tally. Right now, it inherits every factual error from Section 3.2 (wrong prediction range, wrong correction factor, wrong source station) and adds its own (−29.1 nT unverifiable claim, cross-reference error in DOCX, HTML/DOCX divergence).

The strategic problem: this section will be under maximum scrutiny in August 2026 when the eclipse actually occurs. Both communities will compare our critique against what the dome actually predicted. If we say '−17 to −21 nT derived from 1.672× GUA 2016 data' and the dome's actual published prediction is a 9-station dual-baseline system with FSF factors from H(r)/r geometry, we look like we didn't read the predictions page. This is especially damaging because Section 6.2 is in the Conclusions tab — it's the version of the argument most readers will see.

The fix is straightforward: (1) describe the dual-baseline 9-station system accurately, (2) note that the combined prediction range (−5 to −26 nT) covers nearly all plausible outcomes, (3) note the E-PRED tests and explain why they don't uniquely discriminate, (4) keep the Kp escape clause analysis but add the standard-practice qualifier, (5) either source the −29.1 nT claim precisely or replace it with the verifiable observation that W004-baseline predictions (e.g., HAD at −26.2 nT) diverge significantly from the homepage's −17 to −21 nT framing.

The DOCX cross-reference error (Part 3.1 instead of Part/Section 3.2) is a simple but embarrassing bug that should be fixed immediately.

citations_unchecked: Chapman (1933) — referenced indirectly via 'peer-reviewed since 1933'; standard attribution, pre-DOI
The −29.1 nT computation claim — cannot be verified without access to the dome's actual monitor.py source code
The '60% of days have Kp ≥ 2' statistic — plausible but uncited, needs NOAA SWPC source
